elements.sent-2411-001: The Laser Interferometer Space Antenna (LISA) mission is being developed by ESA with NASA participation.
elements.sent-2411-002: As it has recently passed the Mission Adoption milestone, models of the instruments and noise performance are becoming more detailed, and likewise prototype data analyses must as well.
elements.sent-2411-003: Assumptions such as Gaussianity, Stationarity, and continuous data continuity are unrealistic, and must be replaced with physically motivated data simulations, and data analysis methods adapted to accommodate such likely imperfections.
elements.sent-2411-004: To this end, the LISA Data Challenges have produced datasets featuring time-varying and unequal constellation armlength, and measurement artifacts including data interruptions and instrumental transients.
elements.sent-2411-005: In this work, we assess the impact of these data artifacts on the inference of Galactic Binary and Massive Black Hole properties.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our analysis shows that the treatment of noise transients and gaps is necessary for effective parameter estimation.
elements.sent-2411-007: We find that straightforward mitigation techniques can significantly suppress artifacts, albeit leaving a non-negligible impact on aspects of the science.

elements.sent-2411-010: Introduction By measuring \glspl{GW in milliHertz frequencies \gls{LISA will initiate a revolution comparable to the advent of infrared astronomy in the middle of the nineteenth century.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Scheduled for launch in the mid-2030s, it will be sensitive to wavelengths ten thousand times larger than what we can currently observe from the ground.
elements.sent-2411-012: The LISA \gls{GW sources will mainly be binaries of compact stars in our Galaxy, emitting quasi-monochromatic \glspl{GW .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Additionally, there is a high probability to observe transient gravitational radiation coming from the merger of distant \glspl{MBHB with masses between $10^4$ and $10^7$ solar masses~\cite{colpi2024lisa , \glspl{EMRI , and stochastic backgrounds of \glspl{GW from cosmological or astrophysical origins.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: The \gls{ESA adopted the mission in January 2024, kicking off the full design and implementation of the instrument and spacecraft.}
elements.sent-2411-015: To prepare the mission adoption review, many studies were conducted regarding instrumentation, calibration, noise reduction, and data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-016: Among these, one team was responsible for assessing the robustness of the data analysis in the presence of data artifacts.
elements.sent-2411-017: The aim was to evaluate whether the mission science objectives could still be met in the presence of instrumental disturbances in the measurement process.
elements.sent-2411-018: In this article, we report the main findings of this pre-adoption study.
elements.sent-2411-019: The \gls{LISA measurements will be affected by various instrumental perturbations on top of the Gaussian stationary noise characterized by the \gls{PSD function derived from the performance model.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Based on the results obtained by the \gls{LPF precursory mission, we foresee that two major instrumental artifacts impacting LISA will be gaps and glitches.}
elements.sent-2411-021: The former are either temporary corruptions or complete interruptions of the science data stream, as those occurring at various points in the \gls{LPF data~\cite{PhysRevLett.120.061101,10.1093/mnras/stz1017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: The latter are spurious instrumental transients arising in the data, as the force events transferring impulse to the \glspl{TM the case of \gls{LPF ~\cite{PhysRevD.106.062001 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Some disturbances can be anticipated during flight, such as planned interruptions for routine observatory maintenance, while others appear randomly in the measurement process.
elements.sent-2411-024: It is paramount to evaluate their impact on extracting scientific information, and to which extent they can be mitigated.
elements.sent-2411-025: Several approaches have been proposed to limit the effect of artifacts in the data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-026: Considerable efforts have been made to detect, identify and classify glitches in terrestrial detectors, often based on machine learning (see, for example, \cite{robinet_omicron_2020,Colgan2023,Alvarez-Lopez_2024 ).}
elements.sent-2411-027: Once detected, glitches can be modeled alongside the \gls{GW signals to avoid bias in signal characterization~\cite{Bayeswave,Ashton2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: This methodology has also been investigated to process future LISA measurements using wavelets~\cite{Robson2019 , parametric models and shapelets~\cite{spadaro2023glitch , or machine learning~\cite{houba_detection_2024 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Concerning data gaps, special processing methods have also been devised in the context of ground-based detectors both in the case of masked disturbances~\cite{Zackay2021 and the absence of data~\cite{Dreissigacker2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: However, they may be more challenging to deal with in LISA measurements where continuous sources are guaranteed and transient sources are observed over months.
elements.sent-2411-031: To mitigate their impact, data imputation techniques like Bayesian data augmentation~\cite{Baghi_2019 , sparse inpainting~\cite{blelly2021sparse , or autoencoders~\cite{mao_novel_2024 have been prototyped.}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The purpose of this work is not to introduce novel techniques to handle data artifacts.
elements.sent-2411-033: Rather, it firstly aims to assess the impact of instrumental disturbances on standard analysis techniques, with minor modifications to mitigate them.
elements.sent-2411-034: In this perspective, we address the Spritz \gls{LDC .}
elements.sent-2411-035: As discussed above and in our conclusions, more advanced techniques have been proposed to deal with instrumental artifacts, and need to be further developed and incorporated in LISA global fit.
elements.sent-2411-036: Here, we report mainly on results that have been derived before LISA was adopted.
elements.sent-2411-037: This study is based on simulated datasets released in the context of the publicly available \glspl{LDC ~\footnote{\url{https://lisa-ldc.lal.in2p3.fr/ , a set of simulations aiming at testing data analysis pipelines to extract \gls{GW with LISA~\cite{baghi2022 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: The \gls{LDC -2b challenge, nicknamed Spritz, currently is the only one that features noise artifacts in the form of gaps and glitches~\cite{spritz .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: It is split in two main sub-challenges based on the included \gls{GW source: the first one includes \glspl{VGB , compact binary star systems that have been identified by electromagnetic observations to be potential \gls{LISA source candidates (although this is under debate, see \cite{littenberg2024 ).}}}}

elements.sent-2411-066: In the context of \gls{LISA , a gap in the data stream is either due to missing telemetry or to a data portion highly contaminated by noise to the extent that it cannot be used in the data analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-067: As mentioned above, in this study we discard the gap pattern of the publicly available datasets in favor of a more realistic realization of the gap pattern tied to the mission duty cycle, set near 82\%~\cite{colpi2024lisa .}
elements.sent-2411-068: The one implemented here is a plausible realization of a gap pattern including planned short-duration and unplanned long-duration data gaps.
elements.sent-2411-069: Planned short-duration data gaps may occur when the measurements are too disturbed by maneuvers to be used for scientific purposes, e.g. during the periodic re-pointing of the high gain antenna towards Earth to establish communication and telemetry data, or the rotation of the \gls{PAAM to correctly align the transmitting and receiving laser beams.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Planned gaps will not be perfectly periodic, but will have~almost constant cadence.
elements.sent-2411-071: Planned gaps may have moderate durations, such as are likely during antenna re-pointing gaps, lasting a few hours every 14 days, and the shorter duration \gls{PAAM gaps lasting no more than 100 seconds with a rate of at most a few events per day~\cite{colpi2024lisa .}}
elements.sent-2411-072: The true set of planned interruptions will depend on final spacecraft and instrument design and operational details, but the pattern used in this analysis is designed to be plausible yet conservative.
elements.sent-2411-073: Unplanned long-duration gaps are instead unpredictable interruptions in the data stream, caused by anomalies such as those observed in \gls{LPF , e.g. recovery safe modes from spacecraft or instrument failures (such as computer crashes), and micrometeorite impacts~\cite{Thorpe_2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-074: In our plausible yet conservative scenario, they can occur several dozens of times a year, and can last from one to three days.

elements.sent-2411-082: Glitch mitigation strategy In this section we discuss how glitches impact parameter estimation without mitigation.
elements.sent-2411-083: We then propose a way of detecting them, and we test a mitigation technique that consists of masking data spans affected by glitches, and treating them as gaps.
elements.sent-2411-084: The strategy we adopt is threefold: i) detect glitches in the \gls{TDI time series through matched filtering, ii) build a data mask from the detection results, iii) compute Fourier-transformed data by applying a smoothed version of the mask to mitigate leakage effects.}
elements.sent-2411-085: To detect glitches in the data we use matched filtering, which requires an analytical model of the \gls{TDI glitch response based on instrumental assumptions and expressed in the frequency domain, and a stationary noise PSD model.}
elements.sent-2411-086: The time-domain glitch model assumed in the analysis is the one used for the injection in the \gls{LDC -2b data \cite{spritz , where the glitches are injected as independent Poissonian events with a rate of 4 events per day based on the population analysis ran in~\cite{PhysRevD.105.042002 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Each event results from a momentum perturbation on one of the six \glspl{TM .}
elements.sent-2411-088: The analytical TDI response to the time-domain glitch model is based on the propagation of a glitch signal on a single LISA link \cite{PhDMuratore , extended to the case of TDI second generation combinations.}
elements.sent-2411-089: The noise \gls{PSD model includes the stationary modeled instrumental noise sources for \gls{LISA and the foreground of unresolved \gls{GB sources that will be observable in the LISA band between \SI{0.2 {\milli\hertz and \SI{5 {\milli\hertz .}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: The \gls{GB foreground is modeled based on a population including all binaries with \gls{SNR lower than 7 relative to the total noise budget~\cite{Karnesis2021 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: The resulting stochastic process is non-stationary over time-scales longer than one month.
elements.sent-2411-092: The foreground non-stationarity is not an issue for the one-month \gls{MBHB dataset, but needs to be taken into account in the case of the \gls{VGB dataset: we estimate the noise PSD model for different month-long segments - a duration significantly shorter than the year-long full dataset.}}
elements.sent-2411-093: To implement matched filtering we adopt the same strategy of \cite{PhysRevD.105.042002 .}
elements.sent-2411-094: While in the case of \gls{LPF data only one channel was analyzed, here we extend the method to the multivariate detection problem involving the three \gls{TDI channels $A$, $E$ and $T$.}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Let us call $y_c$ the measured time series in channel $c$ with $c \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ corresponding to $A, E, T$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-096: We use the $\mathcal{F}$-statistics as a detection quantity, \begin{equation}\label{eq:multivariate-f-statistics} \mathcal{F}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{c=1}^{p} \frac{1}{2} \frac{ \lvert \langle h_c | y_c\rangle \rvert^2}{\langle h_c | h_c \rangle},\end{equation} where $h_c$ is the glitch waveform in channel $c$.
elements.sent-2411-097: The waveform depends on parameter $\boldsymbol{\theta = (A, t_0, \beta, I)$ including the glitch amplitude $A$, arrival time $t_0$, damping time $\beta$ and injection point $I$.}
elements.sent-2411-098: We define the dot product as \begin{equation}\label{eq:dot-product} {\langle a_c | b_c \rangle} = \sum_{k={k_{\min}}}^{{k_{\max}}} \frac{\tilde{a}_c(k) \tilde{b}^{\ast}_c(k)}{S_c(f_k)},\end{equation} where $S_c$ is the one-sided noise \gls{PSD in channel $c$, and $\tilde{a}$ denotes the Fourier transform of $a$.}
elements.sent-2411-099: We define the estimated signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) as the square root of the $\mathcal{F}$-statistics: $\mathrm{SNR = \sqrt{\mathcal{F }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-100: When considering $p$ different channels, the quantity $2 \mathcal{F}$ follows a $\chi^2_{2p}$ distribution with $2p$ degrees of freedom.
elements.sent-2411-101: Following the same rationale as in \cite{PhysRevD.105.042002 , we can impose a false-alarm probability $\alpha$ by setting a threshold $F_0$ such that \begin{equation}\label{eq:false-alarm-threshold} P(p, F_0) = (1 - \alpha)^{\frac{1}{q}}\end{equation} where $q$ is the number of parameters we scan and $P(a, x)$ is the regularized lower incomplete gamma function.}
elements.sent-2411-102: To compute $h_c$, we write down a time-domain glitch model assuming a force perturbation on one of the test-masses, integrated into a phasemeter signal in frequency \begin{equation}\label{eq:phasemeter-glitch} v_g(t; A, \beta, t_0) = v_{g,0}(t - t_0 ; A, \beta),\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\label{eq:phasemeter-glitch-2} v_{g,0}(t; A, \beta) = \frac{2A}{\sqrt{\beta}} \left[ 1 - \left( 1 + \frac{t}{ \beta} \right) e^{\frac{-t}{ \beta}} \right] H(t).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-140: Gap mitigation strategy The LISA data analysis method we use operates in the frequency domain, where gaps have the main impact by causing noise frequency leakage inducing spectral distortions, as observed in \Fref{fig:noise-with-gaps-VGB .}

elements.sent-2411-142: However, spectral leakage can be mitigated by applying smooth windowing in the time domain with continuous transitions at the gap edges before the Fourier transform.

elements.sent-2411-144: In the case of continuous sources, the noise power leakage induced by gaps is mitigated by applying smooth windowing to the data, that is introducing progressive transitions at the binary mask edges between zero and one.

elements.sent-2411-258: Conclusions and perspectives We presented a summary of the analyses we carried out to prepare the LISA mission adoption concerning the robustness of current data analysis methods to data artifacts.
elements.sent-2411-259: We investigated the impact of instrumental transient perturbations called glitches, considering \gls{LPF -like force events acting on the \gls{LISA \glspl{TM .}}}
elements.sent-2411-260: We show that completely ignoring their presence in the data analysis is incompatible with meeting the mission's science objectives, especially for continuous sources.
elements.sent-2411-261: The glitches' total power may completely dominate the \gls{GB signal.}
elements.sent-2411-262: As an attempt to mitigate their impact, we use a method that first detects glitches by match-filtering the data using a shapelet model, and then applies a mask to discard the affected data in the analysis.
elements.sent-2411-263: This approach allows us to mitigate most of the impact on \gls{GB parameter estimation, except for low-\gls{SNR sources, for which more significant errors remain.}}
elements.sent-2411-264: For transient \gls{GW sources like \gls{MBHB , glitches induce a bias and increase the estimated uncertainty in classic parameter estimation techniques.}}
elements.sent-2411-265: This calls for the necessity to account for their presence in the data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-266: As a countermeasure, masking yields acceptable results on \gls{MBHB parameter estimation, with a few percent differences in posteriors.}
elements.sent-2411-267: We also investigated the impact of data gaps by considering two different patterns.
elements.sent-2411-268: One represents planned data interruptions, which are already anticipated today and over which we will have some control regarding occurrence times and duration through mission operations.
elements.sent-2411-269: Another pattern represents unplanned gaps, which may occur randomly in the data-taking process and for which no control is possible.
elements.sent-2411-270: As most of today's data analysis methods operate in the frequency domain, we show that the main impact of gaps comes from noise frequency leakage, inducing spectral distortions.
elements.sent-2411-271: If no treatment is applied, or if the frequency-domain covariance matrix is not accounting for them, these distortions may severely impact the characterization of continuous sources like \glspl{GB .}
elements.sent-2411-272: However, applying smooth windowing in the time domain with continuous transitions at the gap edges before the Fourier transform mitigates spectral leakage, provided that the smoothing is optimally chosen in terms of \gls{SNR .}
elements.sent-2411-273: It allows for accurate parameter estimation for most of the \gls{VGB sources we consider.}

Red dotted lines mark the true parameter values: {the \gls{MCMC} search was implemented with equal-armlength waveform approximation, while the \gls{LDC} data include an unequal-armlength LISA constellation \cite{spritz}, leading to a slight bias between the true parameter values and the clean data posterior.}} \label{fig:mbhb-posterior-gaps}
elements.sent-2411-347: They were particularly adapted to the sharp rise and exponential decays featured by the glitches.
elements.sent-2411-348: The model also offers flexibility when glitches exhibit more complex shapes, like double-sided rises.
elements.sent-2411-349: In that case, a linear combination of shapelet functions can be used to represent the glitches.
elements.sent-2411-350: In this work, we added the LISA instrumental response function to this model.
elements.sent-2411-351: To do so, we had to assume an injection point, i.e., a particular origin for the glitch process.
elements.sent-2411-352: To extend the generality of this description, more injection points different from TMs should be tested (leading to different responses).
